events.text: Patient is a 48-year-old White female with a BMI of 27.3 and no significant family history of breast cancer.

At age 47, patient presented with a palpable left breast mass in the upper outer quadrant (C50.4). Biopsy confirmed invasive ductal carcinoma (8500/3), grade 3, ER positive (Allred 7/8), PR positive (Allred 6/8), HER2 IHC 3+, Ki-67 45%.
At age 47, left breast core biopsy: invasive ductal carcinoma, Nottingham grade 3, tumor size 3.2 cm. ER positive 85%, PR positive 60%, HER2 IHC 3+, Ki-67 45%. Lymphovascular invasion present.
At age 47, diagnostic mammogram showed a 3.1 cm irregular spiculated mass in the left upper outer quadrant with associated microcalcifications, BIRADS 5.
At age 47, CT chest/abdomen/pelvis revealed multiple liver lesions (largest 2.8 cm in segment VI) and scattered pulmonary nodules bilateral, consistent with metastatic disease.
At age 47, bone scan showed increased uptake in the thoracic spine T7 and right iliac crest, suspicious for osseous metastases.
At age 47, staging established as cT2N1M1, stage IV de novo metastatic HER2-positive breast cancer with liver, lung, and bone metastases.

At age 47, next-generation sequencing (FoundationOne CDx) identified PIK3CA H1047R mutation, TP53 missense mutation, and MYC amplification. Tumor mutational burden 4 mutations/Mb. Microsatellite stable.
At age 47, initiated first-line THP regimen: docetaxel 75 mg/m2, trastuzumab 8 mg/kg loading then 6 mg/kg, and pertuzumab 840 mg loading then 420 mg, every 21 days.
At age 47, baseline echocardiogram showed LVEF 62%, within normal limits prior to initiation of HER2-directed therapy.
At age 48, after cycle 3 of THP, patient developed grade 2 nausea managed with ondansetron. Grade 2 alopecia noted. Mild peripheral neuropathy grade 1 in fingertips.
At age 48, restaging CT after 4 cycles of THP showed partial response with decrease in liver lesions (largest now 1.4 cm) and near-complete resolution of pulmonary nodules.
At age 48, repeat echocardiogram after 4 cycles showed LVEF 58%, slight decline from baseline but within acceptable range. Continued HER2-directed therapy.
At age 48, after 6 cycles of THP, docetaxel discontinued due to cumulative neuropathy grade 2. Continued maintenance trastuzumab and pertuzumab every 21 days.
At age 48, PET/CT after 6 cycles showed near-complete metabolic response in liver and lung. Bone lesions with decreased FDG uptake consistent with treatment response.

At age 48, patient tolerating maintenance trastuzumab/pertuzumab well. ECOG performance status 0. Neuropathy improving after docetaxel discontinuation. Plan to continue maintenance with surveillance imaging every 3 months.
At age 48, developed grade 1 diarrhea attributed to pertuzumab, managed with loperamide as needed.
At age 49, CT surveillance at 12 months showed continued partial response, stable minimal residual disease in liver segment VI.
At age 49, continued maintenance trastuzumab and pertuzumab. Added letrozole 2.5 mg daily for ER-positive disease given postmenopausal status.
At age 49, echocardiogram showed LVEF 55%, monitored closely. No symptoms of heart failure.
At age 49, multidisciplinary tumor board discussion: patient maintaining good response on HP maintenance plus endocrine therapy. Continue current regimen with close surveillance.
At age 50, CT showed new enlarging liver lesion concerning for progressive disease after 2.5 years on first-line therapy.
At age 50, transitioned to second-line T-DM1 (ado-trastuzumab emtansine) 3.6 mg/kg every 21 days due to disease progression.
At age 50, after 2 cycles of T-DM1, developed grade 2 thrombocytopenia (platelets 68,000). Dose reduced to 3.0 mg/kg.
At age 50, patient reports fatigue and mild nausea on T-DM1 but maintaining daily activities. ECOG 1. Liver function tests mildly elevated, AST 52, ALT 48.

scenario_blurb: Metastatic HER2-positive breast cancer. IHC 3+ or FISH amplified. Treated with trastuzumab-based regimens (THP, T-DM1, trastuzumab deruxtecan). Include hormone receptor status variation (ER+/HER2+ and ER-/HER2+). Some with brain metastases.
